items.question_text: Under what conditions may exceptions to the active-runway coordination requirements be authorized?
items.explanation: The note in 3-1-3 states exceptions may be authorized only as provided in paragraph 1-1-11 (Waivers to This Order) and FAA Order JO 7210.3 paragraph 10-1-7 (Use of Active Runways), where justified by extraordinary circumstances at specific locations. Exceptions are not available through local facility directives alone — they require waiver processes. Even extraordinary circumstances must follow those specific authorization paths.

choices.text: Through the facility manager's verbal authorization whenever extraordinary circumstances exist.
Only as provided in the waiver paragraph 1-1-11 and JO 7210.3 paragraph 10-1-7, where justified by extraordinary circumstances at specific locations.
Any tower supervisor may authorize an exception during low-traffic periods without additional documentation.
No exceptions are permitted — the active-runway coordination rules are absolute and may not be waived.

items.question_text: A controller is about to clear a departing aircraft for takeoff. A known maintenance vehicle is parked on the runway beyond the departure threshold. The vehicle operator has two-way communications and an LOA authorizing RSA operations. What must the controller ensure before the departure begins its takeoff roll?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-5b requires that the runway be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel before a departing aircraft starts takeoff or a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold. This requirement applies regardless of whether the vehicle has an LOA or two-way communications — the runway must be clear for the departing or landing aircraft. The LOA authorizes RSA operations but does not override the runway-clearance requirement for takeoff and landing.

choices.text: The takeoff clearance may be issued because the vehicle has an LOA and two-way communications, satisfying both safety requirements.
Issue the takeoff clearance and instruct the vehicle to hold its position — position-holding is sufficient to meet the runway-clear requirement.
Ensure the vehicle is clear of the runway before the departing aircraft starts takeoff, because the runway must be free of all known vehicles, equipment, and personnel before takeoff begins.
The vehicle may remain on the runway as long as the pilot is advised of the vehicle's presence before takeoff clearance.

items.question_text: An aircraft performing a wind shear escape maneuver is climbing straight ahead at full power. A second aircraft is 3 miles behind on the same departure route. The second aircraft's pilot has given no indication of any wind shear encounter. What is the controller's correct assumption about the second aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-8d(2) says: unless advised by additional aircraft that they are also performing an escape procedure, do NOT presume that other aircraft in proximity are responding to wind shear alerts/events. The controller must continue providing control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories to the second aircraft. Assuming the second aircraft is also escaping could lead to missed control instructions when the aircraft is actually proceeding normally.

choices.text: Assume the second aircraft is also encountering wind shear and will initiate its own escape — withhold control instructions.
Cancel the second aircraft's departure clearance preemptively because wind shear on the route makes continuation unsafe.
Instruct the second aircraft to follow the escape aircraft's path because wind shear conditions are likely affecting both.
Do not presume the second aircraft is responding to wind shear — continue providing control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories as appropriate.

items.question_text: How is the determination of what constitutes a 'significant disruption' to air traffic operations under the Tarmac Rule handled at different facilities?
items.explanation: The note in 3-1-15 states that facility procedures, including actions that constitute a significant disruption, vary by airport and must be identified in the facility directive pertaining to the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule. There is no universal definition — each airport defines what 'significant disruption' means in its own facility directive, because disruption thresholds depend on local runway configuration, traffic volume, and gate availability.

choices.text: Facility procedures, including what constitutes a significant disruption, vary by airport and must be identified in each facility's Tarmac Rule directive.
A significant disruption is defined uniformly by the DOT rule and applies identically at every U.S. airport.
The controller on duty determines what constitutes significant disruption on a case-by-case basis without referencing any directive.
Significant disruption means any delay exceeding 15 minutes and is not subject to facility-level interpretation.

items.question_text: A pilot asks the tower for the current FICON NOTAM for Runway 36. The controller is handling moderate traffic. What is the controller's obligation regarding this request?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-3-1e(2) states: 'Issue FICON NOTAMs upon pilot request, workload permitting.' The controller must issue the FICON NOTAM when requested by a pilot, but the obligation is qualified by workload — the controller issues it to the extent the traffic situation allows. The request does not need to come from an arriving or IFR aircraft specifically.

choices.text: The controller must issue the FICON NOTAM immediately regardless of workload — pilot requests for NOTAMs are always mandatory and have priority over separation duties.
The controller may never issue FICON NOTAMs — only the airport operator or flight service station may provide NOTAM information.
Issue the FICON NOTAM upon pilot request, workload permitting — it is an on-request service, not an automatic broadcast, and workload is a qualifying factor.
FICON NOTAMs are issued only to IFR aircraft on an instrument approach and only without a pilot request.

items.question_text: Runways 18L and 18R are parallel runways served by separate ILS systems. Runway 18R is closed by NOTAM. Under what condition may the ILS associated with the closed runway 18R still be used for approaches to runway 18L?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-3-2c states that the ILS associated with the closed runway should not be used for approaches UNLESS not using the ILS would have an adverse impact on the operational efficiency of the airport. The default rule is 'do not use' but there is an efficiency exception. The side-step maneuver exception in paragraph 4-8-7 is a separate authorization.

choices.text: Never — the ILS for a closed runway may not be used for any purpose until the runway reopens.
Only when the approach will be a practice instrument approach that does not result in a landing on any runway.
Whenever the pilot requests it, because pilot preference overrides the closed-runway ILS restriction.
Only when not using the ILS would have an adverse impact on the operational efficiency of the airport, per the exception in 3-3-2c.

items.question_text: A NOTAM is in effect closing Runway 27 for construction. The construction supervisor asks the tower to turn on Runway 27's edge lights so workers can see the runway surface area at dusk. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-4-10e says: 'Do not turn on the runway edge lights when a NOTAM closing the runway is in effect.' However, the accompanying note states that the application concerns use for takeoffs/landings/approaches and does NOT preclude turning lights on for use of unaffected portions of a runway for taxiing aircraft, surface vehicles, maintenance, repair, etc. So for maintenance work on a closed runway, the lights may be operated.

choices.text: Yes — the NOTAM restriction on lighting applies to takeoffs, landings, and approaches, but does not preclude lighting for maintenance, repair, or surface vehicle use.
No — when a NOTAM closes the runway, runway edge lights must never be operated under any circumstance.
Yes, but only between sunset and sunrise — nighttime maintenance lighting on closed runways is the only authorized exception.
No — the runway may only be illuminated by portable construction lighting brought in by the maintenance crew.

items.question_text: A pilot requests that runway edge lights be turned off for Runway 18 because the lights are causing glare during a daytime approach. No other aircraft are using or will be affected by the change. Can the controller comply?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-4-10d(2) permits operating lights differently from the standard rules when requested by a pilot and no other known aircraft will be adversely affected. This applies to both turning lights on and off. The note also explicitly acknowledges that pilots may request lights to be turned on or off contrary to the standard subparagraphs.

choices.text: No — runway edge lights must always follow the standard sunrise/sunset and visibility rules without exception.
Yes — the controller may turn the lights off as pilot-requested because it will not adversely affect other aircraft and the rule permits pilot-requested deviations.
Yes, but only if the facility directive contains a specific provision authorizing on-request light deactivation.
No — pilots may only request lights to be turned ON, not OFF, under paragraph 3-4-10.

items.question_text: A controller must select the runway in use. Which factor takes precedence when choosing among available runways?
items.explanation: The runway most nearly aligned into the wind is the primary consideration when selecting the active runway, provided it is operationally suitable. Other factors like runway length, approach aids, and traffic flow are secondary considerations. The wind alignment serves safety by minimizing crosswind components for arriving and departing aircraft.

choices.text: The runway that yields the highest airport acceptance rate for the current traffic demand.
The longest available runway, regardless of wind direction.
The runway most nearly aligned into the wind, when operationally suitable.
The runway preferred by the majority of pilots on frequency at the time of selection.

items.question_text: A pilot asks the tower about declared distances for Runway 18. How should the controller respond?
items.explanation: The note in 3-7-1 explains that declared distances are published in the Chart Supplement or AIP for pilot preflight planning only. They cannot be used for any ATC purpose. There is no requirement for facility personnel to be aware of them. If pilots inquire, the controller refers them to the Chart Supplement or AIP — they are not used for clearances, separation, or intersection departure advisories.

choices.text: Issue the declared distance for the runway because pilots are entitled to this information as part of ATC services.
State that declared distances are not a real concept in FAA documentation and advise the pilot to use the published runway length instead.
Look up the declared distance in your facility directive and issue it, because controllers must know all published distances for their runways.
Refer the pilot to the Chart Supplement or AIP — declared distances are for pilot preflight planning and cannot be used for any ATC purpose.

items.question_text: An aircraft is taxiing on Taxiway A approaching Runway 18. The tower observes another aircraft converging toward the same intersection from Taxiway B. Both aircraft are following their assigned routes. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: When aircraft on different taxiways are converging toward the same intersection, the controller must issue instructions to ensure separation — either by holding one aircraft short or by providing specific sequencing instructions. The controller maintains positive control over all aircraft on the movement area. Simply letting them 'see and avoid' at a taxiway intersection is not sufficient when both are under ATC control.

choices.text: Issue instructions to hold one aircraft short or provide sequencing to ensure they do not conflict at the intersection.
Allow both aircraft to continue because they are on different taxiways and the pilots will see each other at the intersection.
Switch both aircraft to the same frequency and instruct them to coordinate with each other.
Issue a general traffic advisory and let the pilots decide how to handle the intersection crossing.

items.question_text: A departing pilot calls and states 'have the numbers.' What departure information may the controller omit based solely on the 'have the numbers' statement?
items.explanation: 'Have the numbers' permits omission of runway, surface wind, and altimeter only. It does NOT indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast. Any other ATIS-departure information (such as NOTAMs, braking action, RwyCC) must still be issued unless the pilot separately states the ATIS code. The two omission rules are independent — 'have the numbers' and ATIS code trigger different omission scopes.

choices.text: All ATIS departure information may be omitted — 'have the numbers' is equivalent in effect to stating the ATIS code.
Runway, surface wind, and altimeter only may be omitted — 'have the numbers' triggers a narrower omission than the ATIS code covers.
Only the altimeter setting may be omitted because 'have the numbers' refers exclusively to altimeter information.
No departure information may be omitted — 'have the numbers' has no omission effect for departures and applies only to arriving aircraft.

items.question_text: A controller issues a 'line up and wait' instruction to a departing aircraft. Before the departure clearance can be issued, an emergency aircraft declares a priority landing on the same runway. What must the controller do with respect to the waiting aircraft?
items.explanation: When an aircraft is holding in position on the runway (line up and wait) and an arriving aircraft needs the runway, the controller must cancel the line up and wait clearance and instruct the waiting aircraft to taxi off the runway. The controller cannot clear the arrival to land with another aircraft holding on the same runway. The line-up clearance must be rescinded before the landing aircraft reaches the threshold.

choices.text: Clear the arrival to land behind the waiting aircraft — the holding aircraft will depart after the arrival passes.
Instruct the waiting aircraft to depart immediately to clear the runway before the arrival lands.
Instruct the waiting aircraft to taxi off the runway and cancel the line up and wait clearance before the arrival crosses the threshold.
Tell the arrival to go around — once an aircraft is lined up and waiting, the runway is committed to that departure.

items.question_text: An arriving pilot reports 'have the numbers' and states an ATIS code of 'Information Golf.' The runway has been shortened by construction. What landing information is the controller required to issue?
items.explanation: The pilot stated the ATIS code, which means ATIS-contained landing information may be omitted. However, paragraph 3-10-1m requires that when the runway has been shortened, the controller must ensure the pilot receives the runway number with a shortened announcement for all arriving aircraft if the pilot does not indicate the ATIS code. Since the pilot DID state the ATIS code here, the shortened-runway announcement requirement in 3-10-1m is not triggered — the ATIS code condition is satisfied. The distinction is that 'have the numbers' alone (without ATIS code) would NOT satisfy 3-10-1m.

choices.text: The shortened-runway announcement requirement is not triggered because the pilot stated the ATIS code — information Golf satisfies the condition for ATIS omission.
Issue the runway number with a shortened announcement and all other landing information — stating the ATIS code does not relieve the shortened-runway requirement.
Issue only the runway, wind, and altimeter because the pilot is clearly unfamiliar with the current ATIS information.
Require the pilot to re-state the ATIS code because 'have the numbers' overrides any ATIS code statement for purposes of the shortened-runway rule.

items.question_text: What is a nonapproach control tower's obligation regarding approach information for arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: Nonapproach control towers must forward approach information to arriving aircraft. This is the information the pilot needs to plan the arrival — such as the type of approach to expect — that the tower has received from the approach control facility. The tower acts as a relay for approach clearance information that the pilot needs before reaching the approach phase.

choices.text: The tower has no obligation because approach information is the sole responsibility of the overlying approach control facility.
Forward approach information to arriving aircraft so they can plan their arrival — the tower relays what it received from approach control.
Issue a full weather briefing and all active NOTAMs — approach information alone is insufficient.
Transfer all arriving aircraft to approach control immediately upon contact so the approach controller handles everything.

items.instruction: What should the ground controller do?
items.situation: Ground control needs to move an airport operations vehicle across an active runway at Taxiway D. The local controller is handling three arrivals on final. Ground coordinates as required: 'CROSS RUNWAY 27 AT TAXIWAY D.' Local approves. The vehicle crosses the runway and is now clear on the other side taxiway. Another ground controller says no further action is needed — the crossing was authorized, and it's done.
items.question_text: What must ground control now do after the vehicle completes the coordinated runway crossing?

choices.text: No further communication is needed because the crossing authorization automatically expires when the vehicle exits the runway.
Wait for local control to call back and confirm the crossing is done before taking any further action.
Transfer vehicle communications to local control for a post-crossing confirmation call.
Advise local control that the coordinated runway operation is complete — verbally or via visual aids as specified in the facility directive.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-3c requires the ground controller to advise the local controller when the coordinated runway operation is complete. This may be accomplished verbally or through visual aids as specified by a facility directive. The completion notification closes the coordination loop and lets local know the runway is available again at that crossing point.

items.instruction: What should the local controller do?
items.situation: You are working local control. A landing aircraft is 3 miles out on final for Runway 18L. You see a vehicle on the runway conducting a surface inspection. The vehicle operator has an LOA and two-way radio communications with the tower. Another controller says to just clear the aircraft to land because the vehicle has an LOA and will exit before the aircraft reaches the threshold.
items.question_text: What must the local controller ensure before the landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold?

choices.text: Ensure the runway is free of the vehicle before the landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold — issue the landing clearance only when the runway is clear.
Clear the aircraft to land immediately because LOA-authorized vehicles may remain on the runway during landing operations.
Issue the landing clearance with a cautionary advisory about the vehicle — the pilot will self-separate.
Tell the vehicle to hold position on the runway and clear the arriving aircraft to land on the remaining runway length.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-5b requires that the runway be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel before a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold. The vehicle's LOA and two-way communications authorize RSA operations up to the runway edge, but the runway itself must be clear before a landing aircraft crosses the threshold. The LOA does not override this requirement.

items.instruction: Build the controller transmission using standard radar traffic-advisory phraseology via CTRD.
items.situation: You are using a CTRD (Certified Tower Radar Display) and need to issue a traffic advisory to an arriving aircraft about another aircraft at their 12 o'clock position, 3 miles ahead, same altitude, also inbound.
items.target_phrase: TRAFFIC TWELVE O'CLOCK THREE MILES SAME DIRECTION ALTITUDE (altitude)
items.explanation: When using a CTRD, paragraph 3-1-6c authorizes the use of standard radar traffic advisory phraseology — the same phraseology used by radar approach control facilities. The format follows the prescribed traffic advisory structure: position relative to aircraft, distance, direction of flight, and altitude.

items.situation: An aircraft departing Runway 27 reports it is performing a wind shear escape maneuver and climbs straight ahead at full power. A second aircraft was just cleared for departure on the same runway and is beginning its takeoff roll. The second aircraft's pilot has not reported any wind shear. Another controller says to instruct the second aircraft to also climb straight ahead because wind shear must be affecting both.
items.question_text: How should the controller handle the second departing aircraft?

choices.text: Immediately cancel the second aircraft's takeoff clearance — wind shear on departure makes any continuation unsafe for all aircraft on that route.
Continue providing control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories — do not presume the second aircraft is escaping unless its pilot reports performing the maneuver.
Instruct the second aircraft to execute the same escape maneuver as the first aircraft — wind shear conditions are likely affecting both departures equally.
Transfer the second aircraft to approach control immediately — the tower can no longer provide approved separation for departures during a wind shear escape.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-8d(2) states: unless advised by additional aircraft that they are also performing an escape procedure, do not presume that other aircraft in the proximity of the escape aircraft are responding to wind shear alerts/events. The controller must continue to provide control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories as appropriate to any aircraft that has not reported an escape.

items.situation: An airport baggage handler who is not on the authorized personnel list tells you there is a large piece of debris on Runway 18. You try calling airport management but get no answer. Another controller says to just broadcast a warning on ATIS — the source doesn't matter when safety is involved.
items.question_text: What is the correct sequence of actions for this report?

choices.text: Confirm the report because the source is unauthorized, and if management remains unreachable, issue a NOTAM publicizing the unsafe condition and inform management as soon as practicable.
Broadcast the report immediately on ATIS without confirmation because any safety report, regardless of source, must be distributed without delay.
Ignore the report because only reports from personnel on the authorized list are actionable until management can be reached.
Close the runway yourself because the debris presents an immediate hazard and management is unreachable.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-3-1c requires confirming information from unauthorized sources. Paragraph 3-3-1d requires issuing a NOTAM and informing management as soon as practicable when management cannot be contacted. Only airport management or military operations can legally close a runway — the controller cannot.

items.situation: A pilot requests to land on Runway 36, which is closed by NOTAM for pavement repairs. You inform the pilot the runway is closed. The pilot persists, saying they have a maintenance issue and need to get on the ground. You quote the applicable NOTAM parts and state a clearance cannot be issued. The pilot insists again, and you judge that the landing would not affect other traffic — no other aircraft are operating near Runway 36.
items.question_text: At this point, what is the correct controller response?

choices.text: Issue a normal landing clearance for Runway 36 since no other traffic would be affected.
Inform the pilot the operation will be at the pilot's own risk — do not issue a landing clearance.
Refuse the request entirely — the three-step procedure ends at step two if the pilot persists after the NOTAM is quoted.
Transfer the pilot to airport management for a runway-use authorization before proceeding.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-3-2b is the third step of the escalating closed-runway procedure: if the pilot insists after being told the runway is closed and after the NOTAM is quoted, AND the controller judges the operation would not adversely affect other traffic, the controller informs the pilot that the operation will be at the pilot's own risk. The phraseology 'LANDING WILL BE AT YOUR OWN RISK' is NOT a clearance — the controller never issues a clearance for a closed or unsafe runway.

items.situation: The wind is 6 knots from 270 degrees. Runway 27 is the preferred wind-aligned runway. However, using Runway 36 would significantly reduce taxi delays because the terminal is on the south side, and a Runway Use Program is NOT in effect. A controller argues reducing delays should take priority because passengers will complain about taxi times.
items.question_text: Which consideration takes precedence in runway selection here?

choices.text: Assign Runway 36 because reducing taxi delays takes priority under the operational advantage exception when no formal Runway Use Program exists.
Let the pilots vote on which runway they prefer — pilot preference always takes precedence over both wind and delay.
Assign Runway 27 — tailwind and crosswind considerations take precedence over delay and capacity, and the wind of 6 knots favors Runway 27.
Assign Runway 18 because a runway with a tailwind component is acceptable whenever it reduces delay.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-5-1c explicitly states that tailwind and crosswind considerations take precedence over delay/capacity considerations and noise abatement. The 6-knot wind favors Runway 27. While operational advantage is a listed exception to the wind rule, it must be weighed against wind safety — and the paragraph says wind takes precedence over delay.

items.instruction: What should the ground controller do?
items.situation: An aircraft is near the runway waiting for departure. Another controller says to clear the aircraft for taxi using the word 'cleared' because it's common practice. Separately, airport operations requests to inspect Runway 18 and suggests using 'the field is yours' since they're FAA vehicles. A military aircraft also requests the remaining distance from an intersection departure point on Runway 18 at Taxiway D.
items.question_text: How should these three surface-movement matters be handled?

choices.text: Use 'cleared to taxi' as a common phrase; accept 'the field is yours' since the vehicles are FAA-operated; tell the military pilot to check the Chart Supplement for the intersection distance.

Use any surface phraseology that's clear; allow airport operations to use unconditional phrases during inspections; decline to provide intersection distance because it's only required upon pilot request.
Use 'cleared' for aircraft but not vehicles; accept unconditional instructions for vehicles only; provide declared distances rather than measured intersection departure length.

Use 'taxi' or 'proceed' instead of 'cleared' for surface authorization; use specific proceed-and-hold-short instructions instead of 'the field is yours'; issue the measured intersection-to-runway-end distance rounded down to the nearest 50 feet to the military aircraft.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-7-1 prohibits using 'cleared' for surface movement authorization (use 'taxi,' 'proceed,' or 'hold'). It prohibits unconditional instructions like 'the field is yours' — specific proceed and hold short instructions are required. Paragraph 3-7-1d requires issuing measured intersection-departure distance rounded down to the nearest 50 feet to all military aircraft unless covered by directives.

items.instruction: What should the local controller do?
items.situation: You have an aircraft holding in position on Runway 27 under a line up and wait clearance — the pilot was told 'Line up and wait.' An arriving aircraft is 4 miles out on final for the same runway. The departure cannot be cleared for takeoff yet because another aircraft just landed on the crossing runway and is still taxiing. The arriving aircraft needs a landing clearance.
items.question_text: What must happen before the arriving aircraft can receive a landing clearance?

choices.text: Cancel the line up and wait clearance and instruct the aircraft to exit the runway — the runway must be clear before issuing a landing clearance.
Clear the aircraft to land behind the holding aircraft — the hold-short line on the runway itself provides adequate separation.
Clear the holding aircraft for immediate takeoff and issue landing clearance to the arrival simultaneously.
Instruct the arrival to go around — a line up and wait clearance commits the runway to the departure regardless of arrival traffic.

items.explanation: When an aircraft is holding in position (line up and wait) on a runway, the runway is occupied. A landing clearance cannot be issued while another aircraft occupies the landing runway. The controller must cancel the line up and wait, instruct the aircraft to exit the runway, and then issue the landing clearance once the runway is clear.

items.situation: An arriving aircraft is on a 5-mile final for Runway 27. The preceding arrival landed, exited the runway, and is now taxiing. Another controller says to issue the landing clearance immediately because the runway appears clear. You look and confirm the runway is clear of all aircraft and vehicles.
items.question_text: What must be verified before issuing the landing clearance?

choices.text: Wait for the preceding aircraft to report clear of the runway on the ground control frequency before issuing landing clearance.
Wait until the aircraft is on a 2-mile final before any landing clearance can be issued.
Issue the landing clearance — the runway is verified clear and approved separation from other traffic exists.
Issue the clearance but only as a 'conditional landing clearance' that becomes effective when the pilot confirms the runway is clear visually.

items.explanation: A landing clearance may be issued when the landing runway is clear and approved separation from other aircraft will exist at the time the arrival crosses the landing threshold. The preceding aircraft has already exited the runway, separation exists, and the runway is clear — the conditions for issuing the landing clearance are met.

items.instruction: What movement instruction should the controller issue?
items.situation: A helicopter requests to move from the ramp to the departure runway. Ground conditions are firm and suitable for surface taxi, and the helicopter has wheels. Another controller says to always use 'air-taxi' for helicopters because it's the preferred method. The pilot has not indicated any preference.
items.question_text: How should the controller handle the helicopter's ground movement?

choices.text: Always issue an air-taxi clearance because the note says air-taxi is the preferred method regardless of conditions.
Use hover-taxi phraseology because all helicopter movement above the surface requires hover-taxi commands.
Tell the helicopter to take off and reposition because helicopters may never taxi on the surface at controlled airports.
Use standard surface taxi phraseology since the helicopter is wheeled and ground conditions permit — air-taxi is preferred but not mandatory when surface taxi is practical.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-11-1a says wheeled helicopters taxiing on the surface use standard 3-7-2 taxi phraseology. Air-taxi is the preferred method when ground operations/conditions permit, but a wheeled helicopter on firm ground can surface-taxi. The controller uses the appropriate movement type based on conditions and need.

items.front: What must the ground controller do after an aircraft or vehicle completes a coordinated runway crossing?
items.back: Advise the local controller when the coordinated runway operation is complete. This may be done verbally or through visual aids as specified by a facility directive.

items.front: Under what authority may exceptions to the active-runway coordination requirements be authorized?
items.back: Only under paragraph 1-1-11 (Waivers to This Order) and JO 7210.3 paragraph 10-1-7, where justified by extraordinary circumstances at specific locations. Local directives alone are insufficient.

items.front: Before a departing aircraft starts takeoff or a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold, what must the controller ensure about the runway?
items.back: The runway must be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel. This applies regardless of whether vehicles have an LOA or two-way communications — the runway itself must be clear.

items.front: What traffic-advisory phraseology may be used when the tower has a CTRD (Certified Tower Radar Display)?
items.back: The standard radar traffic-advisory phraseology prescribed in paragraph 2-1-21 — the same phraseology used by radar approach control facilities. The CTRD enables controllers to issue radar-quality traffic advisories.

items.front: If an aircraft is performing a wind shear escape maneuver, should the controller assume nearby aircraft are also escaping?
items.back: No. Unless those other aircraft specifically advise they are also performing an escape, do NOT presume they are responding to wind shear. Continue providing normal control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories to them.

items.front: What determines what constitutes a 'significant disruption' for the Tarmac Rule at a given airport?
items.back: Each facility's own directive pertaining to the Tarmac Rule identifies what constitutes a significant disruption at that airport. It varies by airport because disruption thresholds depend on local runway configuration, traffic volume, and gate availability.

items.front: What are the three pieces of information required in a RwyCC ATIS broadcast?
items.back: 1) Runway number, 2) the three runway-segment condition codes (e.g., 5-5-3), and 3) the time of the report in Zulu. Example: 'Runway Two-Seven, condition codes two, two, three at one zero one eight Zulu.'

items.front: When does the controller issue FICON NOTAMs to pilots?
items.back: Upon pilot request, workload permitting. FICON NOTAMs are not automatically broadcast — they are an on-request service qualified by the controller's current workload.

items.front: Under what condition may the ILS for a closed runway still be used for approaches?
items.back: When not using the ILS would have an adverse impact on the operational efficiency of the airport. The default rule is that the ILS associated with the closed runway should not be used, but efficiency impact is a valid exception.

items.front: In the closed-runway procedure, is 'LANDING WILL BE AT YOUR OWN RISK' a landing clearance?
items.back: No. It is information — the controller NEVER issues a clearance for a closed or unsafe runway. The pilot operates at their own risk without a clearance.

items.front: May runway edge lights be turned on when a NOTAM closes the runway?
items.back: Not for takeoffs, landings, or approaches. However, the note permits turning lights on for maintenance, repair, surface vehicle use, or taxiing on unaffected portions of the runway — the restriction targets operational use, not all lighting.

items.front: Can a pilot request that runway edge lights be operated differently from the standard rules?
items.back: Yes. Pilots may request lights to be turned on or off contrary to the standard subparagraphs, and the controller may comply if no other known aircraft will be adversely affected. This applies to both daytime and nighttime operations.

items.front: In runway selection, what takes precedence over delay, capacity, and noise-abatement considerations?
items.back: Tailwind and crosswind considerations. Safety of flight related to wind alignment always outranks operational efficiency factors like delay reduction or noise abatement.

items.front: Can declared distances be used for any air traffic control purpose?
items.back: No. Declared distances are published in the Chart Supplement or AIP for pilot preflight performance planning only. They have no special markings, signing, or lighting and cannot be used for ATC purposes. Refer pilot inquiries to the Chart Supplement or AIP.

items.front: To whom must the measured intersection-departure distance always be issued?
items.back: To any pilot who requests it AND to all military aircraft, unless use of the intersection is covered in appropriate directives. The distance is rounded down to the nearest 50 feet.

items.front: When two aircraft are taxiing on converging taxiways toward the same intersection, what is the controller's responsibility?
items.back: Issue instructions to ensure they do not conflict — hold one short or provide specific sequencing instructions. The controller maintains positive control. Simply allowing them to see-and-avoid at a taxiway intersection is insufficient when both are under ATC control.

items.front: If an aircraft is holding in position on a runway (line up and wait) and an arriving aircraft needs to land on that runway, what must happen?
items.back: The controller must cancel the line up and wait clearance and instruct the waiting aircraft to exit the runway. A landing clearance cannot be issued while another aircraft occupies the runway — the runway must be clear before the arrival crosses the threshold.

items.front: What is the operational difference between hover-taxi and air-taxi for helicopters?
items.back: Hover-taxi: slow speed above surface, normally below 20 knots, in ground effect. Air-taxi: expeditious movement from point to point, normally below 100 feet AGL, at speeds above 20 knots. Air-taxi is the preferred method when ground conditions permit. The pilot selects safe airspeed.
